title: Introduction to the Minor Prophets
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Introduction to the Minor Prophets - Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: What Are the Minor Prophets?
subpoints: The twelve Minor Prophets are minor in length, not in importance — they are the Word of God
In first-century Judaism, the twelve were considered one book on a single scroll; this reduced the Old Testament count from 39 to 22 books
Nine of the twelve are directly quoted in the New Testament; all twelve are alluded to in the New Testament
Examples of New Testament quotations: Joel 2 at Pentecost (Acts 2), Habakkuk 2:4 quoted three times in the New Testament, Malachi 3:1 in the Gospels, Micah 5:2 for Bethlehem, Hosea 2 quoted by Peter
The canonical order (as in our Bibles) is not chronological nor strictly by size — the most likely explanation is the arrangement of the scroll

outline.title: The Twelve Minor Prophets Identified
subpoints: Canonical order: Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi
Historical order begins with Jonah (oldest), then Amos, Hosea, Micah, Nahum, Zephaniah, Habakkuk, Obadiah, Haggai, Zechariah, Joel, Malachi
Jonah ministers to Nineveh (Assyria); Amos is from Judah but ministers to Israel; Hosea ministers to the northern kingdom and is asked to marry a woman unfaithful to him
Micah is the Cliff Notes version of Isaiah; Nahum preaches to Nineveh again; post-exilic prophets Haggai and Zechariah deal with rebuilding the temple
Malachi is the last prophet before the four hundred years of prophetic silence until John the Baptist

outline.title: Locating the Prophets in the Timeline of the Old Testament
subpoints: Timeline: Abraham approx. 1900 BC; Moses and the Exodus approx. 1446-1400 BC; Judges and the united kingdom (Saul, David, Solomon — each 40-year reigns); divided kingdom begins when Rehoboam follows the advice of the young men
The first major prophet is Elijah — not a written prophet but one who introduces the prophetic office: proclaiming God's word and calling Israel back to it
The written prophets begin in the 800s BC, when Assyria rises as the dominant power; they appear in the context of the divided kingdom
Less than 2% of Old Testament prophetic text is predictive — most of what prophets did was preach and call God's people to repentance
The northern kingdom fell to Assyria in 722-721 BC; the prophets appear primarily between 800 BC and Malachi around 430 BC

outline.title: Why Study the Minor Prophets?
subpoints: All Scripture is given for our learning — Romans 15:4 and 1 Corinthians 10:11 confirm that Old Testament narratives and prophecies speak to every generation
The prophets' job was to call a rebellious people back to God by using God's Word — this remains the church's task
Understanding each prophet's historical context (which king was ruling, which foreign power was dominant) enables proper interpretation
The minor prophets are not a section of the Bible to skip — they contain the word of God, quoted throughout the New Testament
The series plan: one lesson per prophet, twelve lessons total, starting with Jonah and following historical order

summary:
This Sunday school lesson launches a new series on the twelve Minor Prophets by providing the historical, canonical, and theological framework needed to read them well. Pastor Tito Lyro opens with a reading from Nahum 1:1-8 — the prophet the group will study — and explains that the term 'minor' refers to the length of the books, not their theological importance. All twelve are the word of God; nine are directly quoted in the New Testament, and all twelve are alluded to there.

The lesson walks through an Old Testament timeline from Abraham (approx. 1900 BC) through Moses and the Exodus, the united monarchy (Saul, David, and Solomon each with 40-year reigns), the divided kingdom, and the prophetic era. The first major prophet was Elijah — not a writing prophet, but one who established the prophetic office of calling Israel back to God's word. The writing prophets begin in the 800s BC, as Assyria rises to dominance, and run through Malachi (approx. 430 BC), after which there are four hundred years of prophetic silence until John the Baptist.

The lesson identifies all twelve prophets by name, notes the canonical order (which does not match historical order, most likely for reasons related to scroll arrangement), and briefly characterizes each one. The historical order for the series will be Jonah (oldest), Amos, Hosea, Micah, Nahum, Zephaniah, Habakkuk, Obadiah, Haggai, Zechariah, Joel, and Malachi. The lesson closes by reminding the class that less than 2% of Old Testament prophetic text is predictive — the prophets primarily preached, and that proclamation speaks to the church today just as it spoke to Israel.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Nahum 1:1-8

We are going to start a series on the Minor Prophets tonight. And I want to start by reading just the first eight verses of the book of Nahum. Because they are great verses, but also because it's likely something that we haven't paid a lot of attention to before. We're not going to study them tonight — this is just laying the foundation for future lessons.

In my mind, designing the series, I thought: well, start with a twelve-lesson series, one on each minor prophet. But then I thought, we need to situate them, we need to locate them — where are they in the history of the Bible? And how can we understand them in the context in which they are found? And I thought: to do that, we need to figure out what is the context of the Bible — the Old Testament at least. And where it fits — not necessarily the content, but where it fits in history. So we can be ready to understand each of these minor prophets in their own context.

So let's look at Nahum chapter 1, verses 1 through 8. This is the word of our Lord.

*The burden against Nineveh. The book of the vision of Nahum the Elkoshite. God is jealous, and the Lord avenges; the Lord avenges and is furious. The Lord will take vengeance on His adversaries, and He reserves wrath for His enemies. The Lord is slow to anger and great in power, and will not at all acquit the wicked. The Lord has His way in the whirlwind and in the storm, and the clouds are the dust of His feet. He rebukes the sea and makes it dry, and dries up all the rivers. Bashan and Carmel wither, and the flower of Lebanon wilts. The mountains quake before Him, the hills melt, and the earth heaves at His presence — yes, the world and all who dwell in it. Who can stand before His indignation? And who can endure the fierceness of His anger? His fury is poured out like fire, and the rocks are thrown down by Him. The Lord is good, a stronghold in the day of trouble; and He knows those who trust in Him. But with an overflowing flood He will make an utter end of its place, and darkness will pursue His enemies.*

Good feelings or bad feelings about that? Now, has anybody ever heard a sermon on Nahum? Most of you should raise your hand — because a couple of years ago, a member of our congregation preached a sermon from this very passage on Nahum 1:7. So a lot of you have heard at least one sermon on Nahum.

## What Are the Minor Prophets?

Now, the Minor Prophets are not super well known. They're not called minor because they're minor — as if we're just going to look at the major leagues. The word "minor" in the name comes from length, not from importance. There are lots of important things in the Minor Prophets — because what are they? They are the word of God. They are the B-I-B-L-E.

Somebody, I said that once a few weeks ago, and somebody who listened to the sermon online contacted me trying to figure out what version I was using — the BIBLE version. So I told them: write it down. Now read it. So it's not a special version. The B-I-B-L-E. That's the book for me.

So tonight's lesson is not going to be warm and fuzzy. It's not going to be the type of lesson where you learn a lot of how-to-do-life sorts of things. But it's one of those lessons you need in order to be able to put the puzzle together as we study the Bible.

## Can You Name the Twelve?

First question for the evening: can you list the twelve Minor Prophets? Let's see — Jonah. Micah. Zephaniah. Obadiah. Hosea. Zechariah. Habakkuk. Haggai. Amos. Joel — oh, that was already said. Nahum. And the last one: Malachi.

So these are the twelve. These are the twelve Minor Prophets in canonical order — that is, the order they are found in the Bible. They were not necessarily written in this order. And when I was being licensed to be a pastor, I had big tests to take. One of the oral questions asked me to give the twelve Minor Prophets in historical order, with a domestic and foreign king for each one of them. At the time I was able to do it. I can no longer do that — at least for the kings.

Now, first-century Judaism considered these twelve as one book. So in the Jewish Bible of the first century, there were only twenty-two books instead of our thirty-nine in the Old Testament. One of the reasons is because these twelve were one book — one scroll. And then first and second Chronicles were one scroll, and first and second Kings were one scroll, and so on. So you can see how they got to twenty-two instead of thirty-nine.

Nine of them are directly quoted in the New Testament: Hosea, Joel, Amos, Jonah, Micah, Habakkuk, Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi. And all twelve are alluded to in the New Testament.

Can you think of a place where any of these are quoted? Joel 2 is the text of Peter's Pentecost sermon in Acts chapter 2 — that's what he uses to explain what's going on. The just shall live by faith — that's from the prophet Habakkuk, and it's quoted three times in the New Testament. The messenger of the covenant — that's Malachi, and it's quoted in the Gospels. Hosea is quoted by Peter: you are my people, you are not my people, but now you are my people. And Micah — "But you, Bethlehem Ephrathah" — the prophecy about the Messiah being born in Bethlehem, quoted in the Gospels.

So you can see that this is not a section of the Bible that the apostles or Jesus glossed over. These are important books in the history of Christian theology.

## The Canonical Order

The twelve are listed in this canonical order in the Bible. But Sanya asked a great question: if they're not in chronological order, what kind of ordering is it? The most prevalent theory is by the size of the scroll. When you copy a scroll, you do not want to start a book on the same column as another one ended — you want better divisions as they are copied. So they figured out an order where you'd have better divisions. And size explains most of it, though not all of it.

## Timeline of the Old Testament

This is a timeline of the Old Testament. If you are homeschooling parents or involved in classical Christian education in some way, timelines are your thing. And I think timelines can be helpful.

We have Abraham coming around 1900 years before Christ. These are somewhat approximated dates — you'll find different sources a little earlier or a little later. At this point there's not much going on in the world. The Romans don't exist. The Greeks barely exist. Romulus and Remus haven't been found by the wolves yet. There is no Rome, there is no Greece — and we're already here as the people of God.

Jacob, Joseph, and then they go to Egypt. The 400 years of slavery. Moses comes — Sinai somewhere between 1500 and 1400 BC. The conservative scholarship puts it around 1446 BC. The liberals push it to around 1200 BC. And you may have seen the Prince of Egypt movie — there are a lot of accurate things and a lot of inaccurate things in it. The liberal timeline puts Rameses as the Pharaoh, but the evidence actually points to Moses writing with an early alphabet, which was in use as far back as 1800 BC, in the Nile Delta, in what we might call Proto-Hebrew.

Then Joshua, the period of Judges, and then the monarchy: Saul, David, Solomon — all with forty-year reigns. In God's good providence, all three equal lengths. So far, there is no written prophet. The first major prophet in the kingdom years is not a writing prophet — it is Elijah. He kind of introduces the office of the prophet, whose job is to proclaim God's word to the people and call God's people back to the word of God.

That's why we read the prophets and find them seemingly so negative. It seems like so much judgment and doom. Well, because their job is to call a people who are rebellious against God back to God by using God's word. Less than 2% of what the prophets say is about telling the future. Most of what prophets did was preach — not necessarily tell about the future.

The prophets don't really come until the divided kingdom. Remember what happened? Solomon was the last king to reign over all Israel. Then his son Rehoboam came, and he listened to the young people. He literally listened to the young people instead of the elders. The young men said: don't ease your father's burden — add to it. And Rehoboam said: my little finger is going to be thicker than my father's thigh. And the people said: see ya. Ten tribes left. He stayed with a tiny little kingdom — really just Judah and Benjamin and the areas around Jerusalem. And that is the beginning of the divided kingdom.

That divided kingdom continues all the way to the Assyrian captivity of the northern kingdom. The ten northern tribes were taken away by Assyria in 722-721 BC. The Assyrians — where is the capital of Assyria? Nineveh. That's where the capital of Assyria is. And that's when we start seeing the written prophets: in the 800s BC, with Amos, Micah, and Isaiah.

So that's where the prophets fit in the history of Israel — as Assyria rises and Israel rebels, and God calls them back through the prophets' proclamation of his word.

And then right here with Malachi, we have the last written prophet of the Old Testament. And from Malachi all the way until John the Baptist comes, there is silence from heaven. And they don't hear anything from the Lord. And that's a lot of time — about four hundred years. But it was not unusual to go long periods without direct revelation. How many times does the Bible tell us Abraham actually heard from God directly? Five times in a long life. We can open the Bible any day, any moment of any day, and hear from God right then. We are in a better time than Abraham.

## The Twelve Minor Prophets and Their Ministries

Let me briefly characterize each of the twelve, in historical order for the series.

**Jonah** is the oldest of the prophets. He is a prophet not to Israel but to Nineveh — Assyria. He is sent there as a prophet who didn't want to go, tried to run away, and eventually the entire city experienced an awakening and an awareness of their sin and the presence of God. One thing about Jonah everyone knows: the fish. Not a whale — a great fish. And remember: Jonah is in the Bible. By the way, the name John in the New Testament, if it were written in Hebrew, would be Jonah. So the Apostle John is actually Jonah.

**Amos** is interesting because he is from Judah but ministers to Israel — he crosses the border. He is a shepherd with a series of visions. You know the shepherd. And he has a series of visions that somewhat remind you of Joseph's visions in Genesis.

**Hosea** ministers to the northern kingdom. One thing you know about Hosea: the Lord asked him either to marry a woman who had committed herself to prostitution or to keep on living with a wife who became a prostitute. He had to name his kids memorable names: Lo-Ruhamah (No Mercy) and Lo-Ammi (Not My People).

**Micah** ministers to Judah. The great thing about Micah is that it is Cliff Notes for Isaiah — in only nine chapters. If you are too lazy to read Isaiah, read Micah and you will have a good idea of what Isaiah is saying.

**Nahum** also preaches to Nineveh — again. A different generation.

**Zephaniah** ministers to Judah. **Habakkuk** to Judah as well.

**Obadiah** ministers to Edom — the nation that came from Esau.

**Haggai** and **Zechariah** are post-exilic — after the exile. Haggai's main message is about the building of the temple. He says: you have built all your houses and yet the temple is desolate. And Zechariah is apocalyptic — meaning it sounds a lot like the book of Revelation when you read it: the locusts with faces of lions and that kind of thing.

**Joel** — and then **Malachi**, bringing up the rear.

## Why This Matters for Us

No fuzzy feelings, no how-to-live-life applications. But all these things are good for us to have a framework in our minds — where the Bible fits — and then to be able to interpret it more appropriately, because we would not interpret the Bible without reference to the time in which things were written, the occasion that gave rise to the books, and why that particular author decides to write those things.

Remember what Romans 15:4 says: *Whatever things were written before were written for our learning, that we through the patience and comfort of the Scriptures might have hope.* And 1 Corinthians 10:11: *All these things happened to them as examples, and they were written for our admonition, upon whom the ends of the ages have come.*

These books were written for us. They are the Word of God. And they will speak to us as we take them seriously and read them in their context.

Any questions? All right, next Wednesday we start with Jonah — then one lesson on each prophet, following the historical order. They're easy to find in the Bible, which is in canonical order. Just use the table of contents.

Father, I thank you for giving us your Word and giving us the ability to study it. I pray it enables us to be passionate about it. Please be with us as you bless us tonight. In Jesus' name. Amen.
